ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general quesadilla food product class; no beef-specific quesadilla subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP maps to quesadilla as a composite dish; beef-specific variant not separately coded. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef quesadilla as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef quesadillas is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one folded tortilla filled with seasoned ground beef and melted cheese, cut into wedges.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Quesadillas — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Quesadillas (cr_id G00010817). All per-100g macros and available micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (USDA SR Legacy, Beef ground 80% lean cooked / Cheddar cheese composite) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef quesadillas. GI estimated at 52 based on wheat flour tortilla (GI ~52, University of Sydney GI Database, white wheat flour tortilla entry). The beef and cheese filling contributes negligible carbohydrate-driven glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × available carbohydrate per serving (36.3 g for 150 g serving) / 100 = 18.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 677 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit beef quesadillas due to sodium content of 677 mg/100 g; if consumed, portion should not exceed 75 g and low-sodium cheese and seasoning alternatives should be used.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (24.2 g/100 g) with low GI (~52) and high caloric density (394 kcal/100 g) requiring careful portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef quesadillas have a low estimated GI (~52) and low sugar (2.0 g/100 g), but the high caloric density and moderate carbohydrate content mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–120 g and prefer whole-wheat tortillas to increase dietary fibre intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~11.2 g/100 g), high sodium (677 mg/100 g), and elevated cholesterol (59 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of beef quesadillas due to the combination of high saturated fat, elevated sodium, and dietary cholesterol; substituting lean ground beef and reduced-fat cheese can meaningfully lower the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; estimated saturated fat ~11 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise beef quesadillas; substituting ground beef with chicken breast or legumes and using anti-inflammatory spices can reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Quesadillas are a Tex-Mex-inspired dish consisting of seasoned ground beef and melted cheese (typically cheddar and/or mozzarella) encased in a grilled or pan-fried wheat flour tortilla. The tortilla is folded or pressed flat, heated until the cheese melts and the exterior becomes golden and slightly crisp, then cut into wedges for serving. The dish delivers a savoury, rich flavour profile with a contrast between the crispy tortilla shell and the juicy, cheesy filling. Per 100 g, beef quesadillas provide 394 kcal, 16.9 g protein, 25.5 g fat (of which approximately 11.2 g is saturated), and 24.2 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 677 mg/100 g, driven by cheese and seasoning, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. Calcium is exceptionally high at 432 mg/100 g due to the cheese content. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 52 (low), based on the wheat flour tortilla component. Clinically, this dish is high in saturated fat and sodium, posing concerns for cardiovascular disease and hypertension management. It is popular as a lunch or dinner item and is widely available in Western-style restaurants and fast-casual dining in Singapore and globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef quesadilla?
Is beef quesadilla high in sodium?
What is the protein content of beef quesadillas?
Are beef quesadillas suitable for hypertension?
How much fat is in a beef quesadilla?
What is the GI of a quesadilla?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010817, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for ground beef and cheddar cheese and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database wheat flour tortilla entry, which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (11.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for ground beef (80% lean, cooked) and cheddar cheese in approximate proportions consistent with a standard beef quesadilla recipe, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010817) nor in USDA FoodData Central composite entries for beef quesadilla as of 2026; limited published data exist for this specific traditional preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g (detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB or USDA for this composite dish), polyunsaturated_fat_g (same reason), trans_fat_g (not separately reported for this dish in HPB or USDA composite entries), vitamin_c_mg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; USDA composite entry does not include vitamin C for beef quesadilla), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (B-vitamin panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (same reason), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (same reason), vitamin_b6_mg (same reason), vitamin_b12_mcg (same reason), folate_mcg_dfe (same reason), vitamin_d_mcg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), vitamin_e_mg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), phosphorus_mg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), zinc_mg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), selenium_mcg (not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish), water_g (moisture content not separately reported in HPB Foodo for this dish). Sources consulted and confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) as of April 2026. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on wheat flour tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for beef quesadillas was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; beef quesadillas may or may not use halal-certified beef and cheese depending on preparation. 5. The rheumatoid arthritis condition is included as an additional_condition given the clinical relevance of red meat and saturated fat for inflammatory joint disease.
